q: The tangential velocity of the rotational object is the same for all the particles in it.
In a rotational motion, the object moves in a circle.
In a circular motion, the object moves in a circle.
The greater the tangential speed, the smaller the centripetal acceleration.
The direction of the acceleration is not always the same as that of the change in velocity.
When a rigid body rotates, the rotational plane of each particle is perpendicular to the rotational axis.
In a uniform circular motion, the magnitude of the velocity remains constant.
The motion of the blades of a fan is a circular motion.
Angular velocity is the rate of rotation with respect to the displacement.
When an object, or a rigid body, rotates, it can speed up but cannot slow down.
The centres of rotation of all the objects in a rotating body lie on the axis of rotation.
In a non-uniform circular motion, the angular speed of the object is changing.
The two acceleration vectors $\vec{a}_C$ and $\vec{a}_T$ are perpendicular to each other.
In a uniform circular motion, the direction of the velocity does not change.
In a non-uniform circular motion, an object moves in a circular path with a constant speed.
The tangential acceleration of a rotating object is a measure of how fast the tangential acceleration changes.
The motion of a seat of a Ferris wheel is a circular motion.
When a rigid body moves, the distance between any two points in it remains constant.
The instantaneous angular velocity of the rotational object is the same for all the particles comprising the object.
If a rigid body is in rotational motion, all the particles in it undergo rotational motion.
In a uniform circular motion, the direction of the acceleration is towards the centre of the circle.
When a rigid body rotates, all the particles in it move in a circle.
In a circular motion, the object rotates about its axis.
If the magnitude of the angular velocity of a rotating object changes, the object will have a tangential acceleration.
The direction of the angular velocity vector is along the axis of rotation.
In a non-uniform circular motion, the object does not experience the angular acceleration.
An object that moves in a circle at a constant speed experiences a uniform circular motion.
The greater the radius, the smaller the centripetal acceleration.
For a body in a uniform circular motion, if the time is very small, the change in velocity is directed towards the centre of the circle.
The change in the direction of the velocity constitutes an acceleration.
The motion of the tip of a blade of a fan is a circular motion.
An object moving in a circle at a constant speed does not experience a uniform circular motion.
The equations for constant angular acceleration in rotational motion are analogous to equations for constant acceleration in linear motion.
The motion of a Ferris wheel is a circular motion.
When a rigid body rotates, the centre of rotation of all the points lies on the rotational axis.
The centripetal acceleration is present in both uniform and non-uniform circular motion.
If the magnitude of the angular velocity of a rotating object changes, the magnitude of the linear velocity will also change.
An object revolving in a circle is continuously accelerating, even when the speed remains constant.
The greater the angular speed, the smaller the centripetal acceleration.
A rigid body is a body that does not deform or change shape.
In rotational motion, the object rotates about an axis.
Angular velocity is the rate of change of angular displacement with respect to time.
The tangential acceleration is present in both uniform and non-uniform circular motion.
Angular velocity is the rate of rotation with respect to the angular speed.
A rigid body is a body that deforms and changes shape.
In rotational motion, the object rotates about an axis.
If its angular velocity changes at a constant rate, then we can say that the angular acceleration is constant.
The equations for constant angular acceleration in rotational motion are analogous to equations for constant acceleration in linear motion.
The instantaneous angular velocity $\omega$ of rotational object is different for the particles comprising the object.
Since the direction of linear velocity is tangent to the path, the linear velocity is also called tangential velocity.
The direction of tangential acceleration is always perpendicular to the circular path.
The tangential acceleration of a rotating object is a measure of how fast the tangential velocity changes.
The direction of the angular velocity vector is parallel to the plane of rotation.
When the angular velocity is increasing, the angular acceleration vector points in the same direction as the angular velocity.
When the angular velocity is decreasing, the angular acceleration vector points in the same direction to the angular velocity.
An object revolving in a circle is continuously accelerating, even when the speed remains constant.
Centripetal acceleration is denoted by $\vec{a}_T$.
For uniform circular motion, the linear velocity vector points towards the centre of the circular path.
In nonuniform circular motion, an object is moving in a circular path with a varying speed.
The centripetal acceleration and linear velocity are perpendicular to each other at every point in the path.
The angular speed of the object is constant in nonuniform circular motion.
In nonuniform circular motion, there is tangential acceleration in addition to centripetal acceleration.
Centripetal acceleration is present only in uniform circular motion.
The two acceleration vectors $\vec{a}_c$ and $\vec{a}_T$ are perpendicular to each other.
The resultant acceleration points exactly in the radial direction.
The magnitude of resultant acceleration $\vec{a}$ is, $a = \sqrt{a_c^2 + a_T^2}$.
No matter how the body moves, the distance between any two particles within the rigid body remains constant.
All particles of an object rotating around the axis of rotation move in a linear path.
Angular acceleration $\alpha$ is measured in radian per second squared.
The direction of angular velocity is usually specified by the left-hand rule.
The magnitude of the velocity continuously changes as the object moves around the circle in uniform circular motion.
An object in nonuniform circular motion has a resultant acceleration that is the vector sum of the centripetal and tangential accelerations.
The acceleration $\vec{a}_c$ is in the tangential direction.
The relation between the magnitude of the linear velocity $v$ and the angular velocity $\omega$ of a particle is $v = r \omega$.
